coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric fluorescent biosensor based on catalytic hairpin assembly reaction for ultrasensitive detection of aflatoxin-producing genes
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Tao
Chen, Xin
Sun, Danni
Kong, Dezhao
Wang, Zhirong
Lin, Feng
Li, Yaqi
coredata.dc:description: Aflatoxins as the world's most toxic contamination is metabolized by Aspergillus flavus and its production is closely related to aflatoxin-producing genes. It is significant to monitor the aflatoxin-producing genes as early as possible to prevent the contamination and spread of aflatoxins. Therefore, a label-free and free separation fluorescent biosensor using the selective cation exchange reaction in conjunction with the signal amplification of catalytic hairpin assembly (CHA) was developed for the detection of aflatoxin-producing gene utilizing CdTe quantum dots (CdTe QDs) and Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) as the beacon molecule. There were two types of hairpin structures in this sensing system, hairpin probe 1 (HP1) was generated by Ag+ and the recognition probe with the complex C-Ag+-C structure and hairpin probe 2 (HP2) was the DNA fragments showing a hairpin arrangement. Target nor-1 gene may activate the CHA, which created a significant quantity of HP1-HP2 double-stranded DNAand recycled the target nor-1 gene. The released amounts of Ag + suppressed the fluorescence signal of CdTe QDs, while it wasn't observed the discernible effect on NCQDs. This fluorescent biosensor displayed the excellent sensitivity for aflatoxin-producing nor-1 gene in a dynamic range of 5 pM–50 nM with a detection limit of 1.66 pM. This fabricated sensor also showed the visible distinguish for different concentrations of nor-1 gene. It exhibited the potential to offer vital enhancements for food quality control through a simple, fast and sensitive monitoring test system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescent biosensor
Catalytic hairpin assembly reaction
Ultrasensitive detection
Aflatoxin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of NCQDs and CdTe QDs
Fabrication of the ratiometric fluorescence probes
Morphological characterization of NCQDs and CdTe QDs
TEM and FL analysis of ratiometric system
Recovery analysis of aflatoxin-producing gene in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Gel electrophoresis of CHA (lane 1 to lane 8: DNA marker, nor-1 genes, HP1, HP1-Ag+, HP2, HP1-Ag+/HP2, nor-1 genes/HP1-Ag+, nor-1 genes/HP1-Ag+/HP2).
(A) High-resolution TEM of NCQDs; (B) Lattice fringe of NCQDs; (C) High-resolution TEM of CdTe QDs and (D) Lattice fringe of CdTe QDs.

Circular dichroism spectra of HP1 and HP2 before (A) and after (B) the addition of Ag+ ions; TEM images of CdTe QDs (C) and after reaction with the released Ag+ (D), (E) FL spectra of NCQDs after reaction with the released Ag+; (F) FL spectra of CdTe QDs after reaction with the released Ag+.

XPS analysis after reaction between target DNA and C-Ag+-C XPS pattern (A); High resolution spectra of (B) C 1s, (C) N 1s, (D) O 1s, and (E) Cd 3 d. UV–vis and FL spectra of CdTe QDs (F); Chromaticity coordinate of CdTe QDs (G); XRD pattern of CdTe QDs (H); FT-IR spectra of CdTe QDs (I).

(A) FL of different molar ratios of AgNO3 after reaction with ratiometric fluorescent probes (a) and Folded graph of ratiometric fluorescence ratios (b); (B) FL of ratiometric fluorescent probes with different volume ratios; (C) FL variable of ratiometric fluorescent probes changed by Ag+. (D) Effect of pH value on the ratiometric fluorescent biosensor. (E) Reaction time of this biosensor for nor-1 gene.

(A) The fluorescence emission intensity of NCQDs and CdTe QDs in presence of different concentrates of nor-1 genes; (B) The linear relationship between F629/F445 and nor-1 genes concentration; (C) Selectivity of this fluorescent biosensor; (D) Performing visual detection of nor-1 genes.
Schematic diagram of visual determination of nor-1 genes based on ratiometric fluorescence probes and CHA.
Assay results for detection different concentrations of nor-1 genes in the real maize and wheat samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorescent biosensor based on catalytic hairpin assembly reaction for ultrasensitive detection of aflatoxin-producing genes

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Grain Science and Technology
Jiangsu University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Aflatoxins as the world's most toxic contamination is metabolized by Aspergillus flavus and its production is closely related to aflatoxin-producing genes. It is significant to monitor the aflatoxin-producing genes as early as possible to prevent the contamination and spread of aflatoxins. Therefore, a label-free and free separation fluorescent biosensor using the selective cation exchange reaction in conjunction with the signal amplification of catalytic hairpin assembly (CHA) was developed for the detection of aflatoxin-producing gene utilizing CdTe quantum dots (CdTe QDs) and Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) as the beacon molecule. There were two types of hairpin structures in this sensing system, hairpin probe 1 (HP1) was generated by Ag+ and the recognition probe with the complex C-Ag+-C structure and hairpin probe 2 (HP2) was the DNA fragments showing a hairpin arrangement. Target nor-1 gene may activate the CHA, which created a significant quantity of HP1-HP2 double-stranded DNAand recycled the target nor-1 gene. The released amounts of Ag + suppressed the fluorescence signal of CdTe QDs, while it wasn't observed the discernible effect on NCQDs. This fluorescent biosensor displayed the excellent sensitivity for aflatoxin-producing nor-1 gene in a dynamic range of 5 pM–50 nM with a detection limit of 1.66 pM. This fabricated sensor also showed the visible distinguish for different concentrations of nor-1 gene. It exhibited the potential to offer vital enhancements for food quality control through a simple, fast and sensitive monitoring test system.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data used to support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon request.

ce:section.ce:para: Aflatoxins (AFT) have been recognized worldwide as the most toxic compounds present in nature, they are metabolites of Aspergillus flavus and Aspergillus parasiticus (Guo et al., 2020; Wang, Q. et al., 2023). AFT infects a variety of grain hosts, including sorghum, cottonseed, peanut, and maize (Iqbal et al., 2017). The infestation and contamination lead to AFT contamination not only when the grain is in the growing stage, but also when it is being collected, dried, processed, stored, and transported (Karami-Osboo et al., 2012). Grain contamination with AFT has emerged as a global issue, it was reported that there were more than 25% of the world's food to be destroyed due to the AFT contamination. Meanwhile, tens of billions of dollars are lost annually due to mycotoxin contamination in direct or indirect economic terms (Nsabiyumva et al., 2023; Wang, B. et al., 2023). Additionally, it can result in primary tumors, genetic and chromosomal abnormalities, deformities of the embryo, and potentially fatal acute poisoning in both people and animals (Wang et al., 2019; Xia et al., 2019). 20 separate enzymes and at least 25 related genes are required to carry out the 23 stages in the biosynthesis of AFT (Yu et al., 2004). Several of these enzymes, including nor-1, aflR, ver-1, ord-1, omt-1, and others, have been cloned. The nor-1 gene, sometimes referred to as the aflD gene, can encode a catalase with a molecular weight of 29 kDa that is crucial for the production of AFT (Liang et al., 2015; Zhou & Linz John, 1999). It is valuable for reducing economic losses and maintaining people's health trough detecting the aflatoxin-producing genes to predict and disrupt with AFT production.

The DNA sensor has gained popularity for the detection of microbial infections because of its straightforward operation, excellent sensitivity, high selectivity, and quick response speed (Zhu et al., 2023). An efficient technique to address the current scientific issues with the detection of bacteria that produce AFT is to use DNA fluorescence sensors to identify the nor-1 gene. Catalytic hairpin assembly (CHA) is a powerful and particularly appealing nucleic acid circuit, which is an isothermal amplification method for the first time introduced and reported by Yin group (Yin et al., 2008). Two complementary DNA strands that have been produced as stable hairpin structures are frequently employed in CHA reactions. The hairpin stems were used to imprison the complementary domains, which kinetically prevents spontaneous contact between the two DNA strands. As a result of the toehold nature of single-stranded nucleic acids, the two hairpins were opened sequentially, resulting in the rapid formation of the most thermodynamically advantageous DNA duplexes (Liu et al., 2019). CHA with the advantages of enzyme-free, easy operation, homogeneous reactions media and high-efficiency, has widely applied in biosensing of DNA, small molecule, cell imaging and clinical diagnosis (Li et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2015; Zhou et al., 2017).

The application of probes that utilize C-Ag+-C structures in the detection of Ag+ ions has risen since it was demonstrated the strong specific coordination between mismatched cytosine-cytosine (C-C) and Ag+(Gao et al., 2018; Ono et al., 2008). This recognition mechanism based on cracking the C-Ag+-C hairpin structure to release Ag+ ions as the resultant signals has been used for different targets detection, such as small molecules, proteins and DNA fragment. Meanwhile, the surface of CdTe QDs contained the plenty of Cd2+ which was beneficial to bind Ag + to the QDs surface and quench the fluorescence (Huang et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2021; Wang, Y. et al., 2023). And Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) was reported to have enhanced fluorescence quantum yield, good photostability and resistance to acid or base, which is an ideal beacon material and used for ratiometric biosensor fabrication (Singh et al., 2018; Zhu et al., 2020).

In this work, a novel label-free homogeneous ratiometric fluorescent probe was developed based on the specific recognition response of CdTe QDs in conjunction with Ag+-mediated conformation switch (C-Ag+-C hairpin structure) for detection of the aflatoxin-producing gene by the naked eye. It was shown in Scheme 1 , Ag+ ions binded specifically to cytosine and form hairpin structures (HP1). Aflatoxin-producing genes (nor-1 gene) was triggered to initiate CHA reaction by aflatoxin binding to the loop of hairpin DNA, leading to the release of Ag+ ions and the generation of a double-stranded structure of nor-1 gene/HP1. The released Ag+ ions were then introduced and selectively quenched the red fluorescence of CdTe QDs, and caused a cation exchange reaction with them. The stable internal standard was NCQDs' unaffected blue fluorescence, which did not react with the released Ag+ ions. The hairpin structures of HP2 can be opened by 5’ end of nor-1 gene/HP1 double-stranded structure. Subsequently, the nor-1 gene was displaced by HP2, which was taken participated in the next cycle of CHA. This ratiometric fluorescent-based sensor was fabricated with the high enzyme-free isothermal nucleic acid amplification of CHA, high sensitivity and simple operation to detect aflatoxin-producing genes. Meanwhile, the stability of ratio fluorescence signal of CdTe QDs and NCQDs was fully realized and related color change can be observed by naked eyes, which also created a possible path for the development of visual sensor for target gene fragment detection.

Citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7·H2O), ethylenediamine, cadmium chloride (CdCl2), 3-Mercaptopropionic acid (C3H6O2S), silver nitrate (AgNO3), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), sodium nitrate (NaNO3), magnesium nitrate (Mg(NO3)2), acetone. All chemicals were of analytical reagent grade and used as purchased without further treatment. All the DNA oligonucleotides utilized in this work were provided and purified by Sangon (Shanghai, China). The DNA sequences were heated to 95 °C for 10 min and then cooled to room temperature for at least 6 h for later use. The detailed sequences information for all the DNAs were shown in Table 1 .

Fluorescence emission and UV–vis absorption spectra measurements were performed using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (FS5, Edinburgh) and UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2700, Japan), respectively. Morphological evaluation was performed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Talos-F200X, USA). FT-IR analyses were carried out using an FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific-IS50). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, AXIS ULTRA DLD, Shimadzu, Japan) was used to record the XPS spectra. X-ray diffraction (XRD, D8 Advance, buuker, Germany) was used to examine the crystalline phase in the 2θ range of 10–80°. DNA conformational changes were measured by circular dichroism (J-1500, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of NCQDs and CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The NCQDs were prepared via a one-step hydrothermal method (Zhu et al., 2013). Briefly, 1.05 g of citric acid was dissolved in 20 mL of deionised water under ultrasound, and 340 μL of ethylenediamine was then injected into the solution and stirred with for 10 min. The resulting mixtures was poured in a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave followed by a hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature, the insoluble material was removed using a 0.22 μm filter membranes and dialyzed with molecular weight 3000 Da for 12 h. The obtained NCQDs solution was collected and stored at 4 °C for further use.

The CdTe QDsAGHJHJJJJ were synthesized according to the previous method (Chen et al., 2021; Tang & Li, 2017). 0.0449 g of NaBH4 and 0.0638 g of Te powder were weighed and dissolved in 5 mL of H2O. The solution was nitrogen blown for 10 min and then reacted at 4 °C to obtain a purple supernatant solution of NaHTe. Then 2.5 mL of the NaHTe solution was added rapidly, stirred thoroughly and refluxed at 100 °C for a certain time to obtain MPA-modified CdTe QDs. The solution was cooled to room temperature, mixed with an equal volume of acetone, centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min and the resulting precipitate was freeze-dried. The precipitate was then freeze-dried and prepared as a 0.5 mg/mL solution of CdTe QDs for use in subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the ratiometric fluorescence probes
ce:section.ce:para: By simply combining two quantum dots with distinct fluorescent colors in a certain ratio and thoroughly mixing them under a vortex oscillator, ratiometric fluorescence probes may be made quickly and easily. The steps are as follows: Add 90 μL of CdTe QDs and 40 μL of a specific dilution of blue NCQDs, followed by MOPS buffer for subsequent reactions. The fluorescence emission spectra were obtained in the range of 385–755 nm, with the excitation wavelength set at 365 nm.

According to Scheme 1's instructions for the DNA sensing experiment, HP1 (10 μM, 10 μL) and AgNO3 (100 μM, 7 μL) were denatured at 95 °C for 5 min before being cooled to 25 °C using a similar touch-down technique to PCR, which lowers the temperature by 1 °C every 2 min to generate hairpin structures. In order to achieve a final concentration of 500 nM, the resulting HP1-Ag+ hairpin structure was diluted using MOPS buffer. And then, 60 μL HP2 (500 nM) and 50 μL of the various target DNA were added into the HP1-Ag+ solution, incubation at toom temperature for 1 h to allow for hybridization and the release of free Ag+. For further cation exchange reactions lasting 20 min, the ratiometric fluorescent probes were added to the reaction systems mentioned above. Finally, variations in fluorescence color and intensity were captured on a mobile phone and a fluorescence spectrophotometer, respectively. The standard deviation (SD) was presented as an error bar after each measurement was made at least three times.

To verify the feasibility of hairpin assembly, nucleic acid samples from the reactions were analysed by 1% agarose gel electrophoresis. The samples were analysed by 1% agarose gel electrophoresis. 1 g of agarose was dissolved in 50 mL of 1xTAE buffer, boiled for 1 min and then cooled for 4 min. 5 μL of nucleic acid dye was added and introduced into the gel-making mould. The air bubbles were removed with a dropper and then inserted into the glue-making comb and allowed to cool naturally. Allow to cool. After gel preparation, the gel was transferred to the gel electrophoresis apparatus, 5 μL of the prepared sample was added and the gel was passed through the gel at a constant voltage of 90 V. The gel was then removed and imaged under a gel imager for 90 min. Finally, the gels were removed and imaged under a gel imager.

An enzyme and label-free catalytic hairpin self-assembly ratiometric fluorescence strategy was designed for the quantitative detection of aflatoxin-producing genes. As shown in Scheme 1, HP1 owning the rich cytosine bases was used to interact with Ag+ to form a stable C-Ag+-C metal base pair structure, which became the stem of the hairpin structure.

In the presence of the target DNA of nor-1 gene, it opened the hairpin structure along the loop of HP1 to convert a double-stranded DNA (dsDNA), and released Ag+ into the solution. The sticky ends of the formed HP1/nor-1 gene double strands were recognized and hybridized by HP2 to constitute the HP1/HP2 dsDNA structure, replacing the complete target DNA. The target DNA was released and participated in the next reaction cycle, to generate the more HP1/HP2 structures and release as much Ag+ as possible. The released Ag+ triggered the quenching of the CdTe QDs fluorescence. While it was no effect on the NCQDs fluorescent intensity. It achieved the highly sensitive target DNA detection by measuring the alternating fluorescence signal. Additionally, QD binding to Ag+ triggered a visual color change of the solution, which can achieve visual detection of aflatoxin-producing genes.

This feasibility of the developed CHA strategy was confirmed by gel electrophoresis. It was shown in Fig. 1 , lanes 1–8 are DNA marker, nor-1, HP1, HP1-Ag+, HP2, HP1-Ag+/HP2, nor-1/HP1-Ag+, nor-1/HP1-Ag+/HP2. The lane 6 showed that HP1-Ag+ and HP2 did not react spontaneously when the target nor-1 was not present, thus maintaining their own hairpin structure and existing independently. A new band appeared at the position of lane 7, indicating the formation of a nor-1/HP1-Ag+ complex, demonstrating the specific binding of the target nor-1 and HP1-Ag+. Two new bands appeared in lane 8, corresponding to the complexes nor-1/HP1-Ag+ and nor-1/HP1-Ag+/HP2, demonstrating further specific binding of the target nor-1 to HP2 following the reaction between the target nor-1 and HP1-Ag+ Also, by observing the brightness of the bands showed that nor-1 was dark, demonstrating that the reaction between nor-1 and HP1-Ag+ was sufficient to generate a large amount of signal material for hairpin self-assembly. In addition, lane 8 showed four bands in contrast to the neighboring lanes, corresponding to nor-1, HP1-Ag+, nor-1/HP1-Ag+ and nor-1/HP1-Ag+/HP2. The results demonstrate that when nor-1 and HP1-Ag+ react specifically, further binding of HP2 occurs and HP2 displaces nor-1 from under the nor-1/HP1-Ag+ doublet, setting off again for CHA cascade cycle amplification. These results are consistent with expectations and the electrophoresis results provide strong evidence for a successful enzyme-free CHA-assisted signal amplification reaction triggered by aflatoxin-producing gene recognition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological characterization of NCQDs and CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: High-resolution TEM images of NCQDs and CdTe QDs were displayed in Fig. 2 A and C respectively. The produced NCQDs and CdTe QDs were shaped almost spherically and were evenly dispersed in aqueous solution. No extended sonication was used throughout the preparation procedure, and the images showed excellent dispersion without any evident agglomeration. Additionally, with extreme magnification, the individual quantum dots showed lattice stripes that were clearly visible and whose lattice spacing was 0.212 nm and 0.207 nm (Fig. 2B and D), respectively. This showed that the quantum dots were arranged in a predictable way.

Ag+ has the ability to interact with the cytosines in the HP1 sequence to produce C-Ag+-C metal base pairs, which in turn cause HP1 to form hairpin structures and alter the DNA's conformation. The circular dichroism (CD) technology is a useful tool to examine structural changes in DNA by the phenomenon from circularly polarized electromagnetic radiation to the chiral molecules. Fig. 3 A demonstrates that HP1 contains two peaks, one at 260 nm and the other near 280 nm, which were typical of DNA's B-configuration. The intensity of the negative band of HP1-Ag+ at 260 nm dramatically increased with the addition of silver ions, indicating an increase in DNA helicity and more evidence of C-Ag+-C synthesis (Hossain et al., 2021), with HP1-Ag+. Further research was done into how the target nor-1 addition affected the structure of HP1-Ag+ and HP2. The circular dichroism of HP1, HP2 underwent several alterations before and after the insertion of the target nor-1, as seen in Fig. 3A. The evolution of the intensity of the negative peaks of nor-1/HP1-Ag+ and nor-1/HP1-Ag+/HP2 at 260 nm rose further after the addition of the target nor-1, which typically denotes an increase in the helicity of DNA (Zheng et al., 2014). The negative and positive peaks were also somewhat red-shifted and blue-shifted, respectively, when nor-1 coexists with HP1-Ag+ and HP2. This occurrence demonstrates that the double-stranded structure of nor-1/HP1-Ag+, nor-1/HP1-Ag+/HP2 is created (Fig. 3B), the hairpin structure of HP1-Ag+/HP2 was changed, and the CHA-assisted signal amplification method initiated by nor-1 was successfully carried out.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TEM and FL analysis of ratiometric system
ce:section.ce:para: The target DNA selectively recognized HP1-Ag+ and released Ag+, triggering the cation exchange process, according to TEM analysis. The CdTe QDs that did not react with Ag+ were evenly distributed and did not exhibit any considerable agglomeration, as seen in Fig. 3C. The released Ag+ displayed considerable agglomeration to the CdTe QDs when the target DNA was particularly attached to HP1-Ag+. This was brought on by the cation exchange process between CdTe QDs and Ag+. Additionally, once the target initiated the catalytic hairpin self-assembly, we evaluated the fluorescence intensity of individual NCQDs and CdTe QDs to further support the cation exchange reaction (Hu et al., 2019). Fig. 3D and E shows that the released Ag+ did not impact on the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs, further supporting the idea that NCQDs can serve as a reference signal for fluorescent ratiometric probes. However, the released Ag+ had a severe bursting impact on the CdTe QDs, and a distinct visual effect could be seen by looking at their fluorescence photos (Fig. 3F).

The released Ag+ then integrated with the CdTe QDs in the ratiometric fluorescent probe with a cation exchange mechanism, which was further confirmed by XPS analysis. The catalytic hairpin was activated by the aflatoxin-producing gene by a self-assembly. The Ag 3 d peak can be seen in Fig. 4 A at 371.3 eV (Hu et al., 2019). Both NCQD and CdTe QD characteristic peaks were observed clearly. The complete spectrum in Fig. 4A also revealed the NCQDs peaks at 284.9 eV, 399.8 eV, and 5.5 eV, respectively, which demonstrated NCQDs were mostly composed of C, N, and O element. Fig. 4B displayed the typical peaks of the C 1s, which correspond to C=C, C-C, C-N, and C=O (Liu et al., 2011) at 284.5 eV, 285.6 eV, and 288.5 eV respectively. Fig. 4C showed the characteristic peaks of the N 1s. There were three peaks at 399.4 eV, 407.5 eV, and 402 eV, which correspond to -NH and N-C, N=O, and N-O, respectively (Fleutot et al., 2011). As shown in Fig. 4D, the O 1s was mostly represented by two peaks at 532.6 eV and 536 eV, which correspond to O-H and O=C-O, respectively (Sevilla & Fuertes, 2009). Additionally, The two peaks were primarily represented for Cd 3 d in Fig. 4E, Cd 3d5/2 and Cd 3d3/2 (Jiang et al., 2021), at energies of 404 eV and 407.5 eV, respectively.

The optical characterization of CdTe QDs was a great significant for this biosensor. The UV absorption and fluorescence emission of CdTe QDs was shown in Fig. 4F and located at 583 and 625 nm, respectively. Based on an empirical formula mentioned in the literature (Yu et al., 2003), the average particle size of CdTe QDs can be calculated by the followed formulae:
D =
(
9.812 ×
10
− 7
)
λ 3
−
(
1.7147 ×
10
− 3
)
λ 2
+ 1.0064 λ − 194.84
(D (nm) is the size of CdTe QDs, and λ was the wavelength of the first excitonic absorption peak of the CdTe QDs). The water-dispersible CdTe QDs exhibited a pale pink under the fluorescent light and an intense red color activated by a 365 nm UV laser as shown in Fig. 4F inserted image. The color coordinates of the CdTe QDs at (0.632, 0.363) were displayed in Fig. 4G. The distinctive diffraction peaks of (111), (220), and (311) of CdTe QDs can be seen in Fig. 4H as part of the XRD pattern of CdTe QDs (Liu et al., 2022). The CdTe QDs' IR spectra were displayed in Fig. 4I. The distinctive peak at 1203 cm−1 was brought on by the -COO in the carboxylic acid. 3293 cm−1 was the typical O-H peak of 3-mercaptopropionic acid. The absorption peaks of C=O and COO-, which were induced by the symmetric and asymmetric stretching in the -COO of the carboxylic acid, were 1640 and 1390 cm−1, respectively (Lu et al., 2021). Furthermore, it wasn't observed the absorption peak at 2579 cm−1 from MPA, which demonstrated the mercaptans group was changed into Cd-S coordination bond by covalent binding (Venkatachalam et al., 2021).

The quantity of AgNO3 was first optimized. Inadequate AgNO3 prevents part of the C-C mismatch base pairs from forming fully, which results in non-specific reactions in HP1 and HP2. A proper amount of AgNO3 needs to be screened since excessive AgNO3 results in a high background signal. In order to generate a stable hairpin structure, various volumes of the same concentration of AgNO3 were applied after fixing the quantity of HP1. After incubation with HP2 at room temperature for a certain amount of time, the ratiometric fluorescent probes were then applied, and the fluorescence spectra were examined after incubation. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence intensity of the CdTe QDs grew with the increasing concentrations of AgNO3 from 0 to 7 μL, while it gradually declined from 7 to 10 μL. The optimal volume of AgNO3 for HP1 to form a stable hairpin structure was 7 μL of AgNO3, as seen by the appearance of an inflection point. The fluorescence intensity ratio of the two quantum dots was crucial for both detection and visualization at the same time. In order to study the largest and most consistent fluorescence color change in the visualization experiment, the fluorescence emission of various ratios for the ratiometric fluorescent probe's fluorescence intensity ratio (I639/I449) was studied by fixing the quantity of NCQDs and altering the volume of CdTe QDs and displayed in Fig. 5B. Although the fluorescence intensity of Em (639 nm) increased with the increasing of quantum dot concentration, in order to minimize background interference and to prevent too large a difference in fluorescence intensity for the instrument to measure accurately, the volume ratio of CdTe QDs to NCQDs was selected as 70 μL. Additionally, the FL intensity of ratiometric fluorescent probes was compared by Ag+ exchange and the Changes in fluorescence intensity was shown in Fig. 5C, which further demonstrated 70:1 to be the ideal ratio. Meanwhile, the pH conditions of the reaction environment and the reaction time have also been investigated and displayed in Fig. 5D and E pH 8 and 60 min was the optimal reaction conditions for nor-1 gene detection.

This developed biosensor was used to detect nor-1 genes by measuring the fluorescence intensity of different concentrations of target DNA under the optimal experimental conditions to validate the quantitative test. The fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs decreased gradually with the target DNA concentration increasing, while the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs wasn't seen the change essentially. It was shown in Fig. 6 A and B, the linear regression equation was F629/F445 = −0.1357X+1.2335 (where X was the logarithmic value of the concentration of nor-1 genes) with a linear correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.9997. The limit of detection which was defined through three standard deviations was approximately 1.66 pM (S/N = 3). Comparing with previous reports, this method displayed the better and comparable LOD and linear range than (Table S1). Fig. 6C showed the specificity test, where random, 2-mismatched and 5-mismatched DNA sequences were selected for the experiment. As the number of base mismatches increases, the less effective the bursting of CdTe QDs was, indicating that the ratiometric fluorescent biosensor showed the excellent selectivity.

The previously mentioned reaction system was left to react for a while at room temperature in order to liberate the Ag+, which was then further reacted with the ratiometric fluorescent probe for 10 min at room temperature. Following the completion of the reaction, the components were stacked on the test bench in ascending concentration gradient order with black cardboard serving as the background for the shot. An appropriate angle should be chosen before shining the UV lamp (365 nm) on the feed vial. Use a phone holder to secure the smartphone while you set the exposure level and shoot a fluorescent picture. Fig. 6D performed the fluorescent color of the solution changed from bright pink to weak pink as the amounts of aflatoxin-producing genes increased. As observed in the image, the color of the solution changed when the aflatoxin-producing gene concentration reached 50 pM. When the aflatoxin-producing gene concentration reached 50 pM, the color of the solution substantially altered and could be seen with the naked eye. The color of the solution didn't alter from that of the 1 nM aflatoxin-producing gene until 50 nM. The solution's color was identical to that at 1 nM of aflatoxin-producing gene. The sensor showed a high long-term stability when kept at 4 °C (Fig. S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recovery analysis of aflatoxin-producing gene in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Strains of Aspergillus flavus were inoculated onto maize and wheat samples, cultured for 10 days, ground into powder and extracted their DNA using a DNA extraction kit for PCR amplification to obtained target nor-1 gene. Before the detection, the PCR-amplified DNA was heated at 95 °C for 5 min and was moved immediately into the ice bath for another 5 min to achieve the right structural of target nor-1 gene. Then, the synthetic standard nor-1 gene was added into the treated DNA solution to complete the effectiveness of the constructed ratiometric fluorescence biosensor. The recovery of the three different concentrations of the aflatoxin-producing gene ranges from 91.02% to 107.86 and 94.2%–114.3%, respectively, as shown in Table 2 . The relative standard deviation was between 0.153% and 7.2%, respectively, demonstrating the method's suitability for use in complex agricultural products and offering a fresh approach to aflatoxin testing.

In conclusion, to facilitate the highly sensitive investigation of aflatoxin-producing genes, a homogenous visual fluorescent biosensor was developed based on a selective recognition reaction and an enzyme-free catalytic hairpin self-assembly signal amplification method. The technology just involved combining two quantum dots of different fluorescent colors at room temperature to produce a ratiometric fluorescence probe, which was straightforward to construct and use. It didn't require labeling or separation of nucleic acids. Aflatoxin-producing genes caused CHA to release huge quantities of Ag+, which can interact with CdTe QDs in cation exchange, while having little to no effect on NCQDs' fluorescence. The ratiometric fluorescence probe exhibited a good linearity in the 0.005–50 nM range with a LOD of 1.66 pM. The ratiometric fluorescence system has been designed to provide unique visualization advantages and the ability for semi-quantitative analysis of color changes. With the advantages of high sensitivity, visualization, ease of operation and cost savings of the ratiometric fluorescence probe, this system was expected to contribute to the development of real-time detection in the field.

Tao Yu: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Xin Chen: Formal analysis, Data curation. Danni Sun: Formal analysis, Data curation. Dezhao Kong: Supervision. Zhirong Wang: Methodology. Feng Lin: Validation. Yaqi Li: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31901799, 32001804), the Chinese Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2021M692370).
